steps.teaching_notes: Introduce the soft inch-tape, the tailor's most important tool. It bends around the body, unlike a hard ruler, and reads in inches. Keep it warm: this little tape is where every dress begins.
Measuring the bust: wrap the tape around the fullest part of the chest, keep it level all the way round the back. This is the most important measurement for a blouse.

The waist is measured at the narrowest part, usually just above the navel; the hip at the widest part, standing with feet together. Emphasise saying the numbers aloud in the same order every time: bust, waist, hip -- so nothing is forgotten or swapped.

The tape must sit snug but never pulled tight, and stay level -- not slanting. Warn clearly: pulling the tape tight is the number one beginner mistake, the blouse then comes out too small. A good check: one finger should slip under the tape easily.

Fabric is not the same in every direction. The tight finished edge is the selvedge; the direction along it is the straight grain -- the strongest, most stable direction. Cloth should be cut along the grain.

The diagonal direction (bias) stretches. If a garment is cut off-grain or on the diagonal by mistake, it twists and hangs crooked on the body after stitching and washing. Use a simple analogy: like tearing a chapati along the roll versus across it.

Mark the body line with chalk, then cut a margin of about half to three-quarters of an inch OUTSIDE that line -- that extra strip is the seam allowance, the fabric the stitches will hold. Warn: cutting exactly on the body line leaves nothing to sew with, and the garment cannot be let out later.

At the machine: the needle goes up and down fast, so fingers stay clear; the presser foot holds the fabric; the pedal controls speed -- press gently, start slow. Line the fabric edge against the guide line on the metal plate so the stitching stays a steady distance from the edge.

Let the machine feed the fabric -- hands only guide, never pull (pulling bends the needle and skews the line). At the start and end, stitch a few stitches in reverse (backstitch) to lock the seam so it never unravels.

caption.en-IN: Guide, don't pull; lock the ends
caption.hi-IN: खींचे नहीं; शुरू-अंत में बैक करें

common_mistakes: Pulling the measuring tape tight, so the garment comes out small
Letting the tape slant instead of staying level
Cutting off-grain or on the bias by accident, so the garment hangs crooked
Cutting on the marked body line with no seam allowance
Pulling fabric through the machine instead of letting it feed
Forgetting to backstitch, so the seam unravels

products: Blouse stitching for neighbours (the steadiest local demand)
Alterations: hemming, taking in, zip and hook repair (fast money, no material cost)
School uniforms in bulk before the school year
earning.pricing_notes: Blouse stitching commonly earns Rs 100-300 depending on the design and town; alterations Rs 20-80 each but take minutes; uniforms are lower margin but come in batches of 20-50. The customer usually brings her own fabric, so there is almost no material cost -- her earning is her stitching time.
earning.customer_notes: First customers are neighbours and relatives -- one well-fitting blouse brings five more women. Then: the local tailor shop (overflow work), the school (uniform batches), the self-help-group network and local melas. Being known for fitting well and delivering on the promised day matters more than being cheap.
